title: What's a set?
instruction:
A set is like a bag of unique items — no duplicates allowed. Throw in 5 red balls and 3 blue balls, the bag only remembers "red" and "blue."

Create a set with `{}` or `set()`. Run this and notice the duplicates vanish.
successMessage: Three unique colors from six inputs. Sets automatically deduplicate. The order might vary — sets are unordered.

title: Remove duplicates from a list
instruction:
The most common use of sets: deduplication. Convert a list to a set, duplicates gone.

Remove duplicates from the email list and print the count.
starter:
emails = ["a@b.com", "c@d.com", "a@b.com", "e@f.com", "c@d.com", "a@b.com"]

unique = list(set(emails))
print(f"{len(emails)} total, {len(unique)} unique")
successMessage: list → set → list. One line removes all duplicates. You'll use this pattern constantly when processing data.

title: Add and remove
instruction:
`.add(item)` adds one item. `.discard(item)` removes it (silently does nothing if missing). `.remove(item)` raises an error if missing.

Use `.discard()` when you're not sure if the item exists — safer than `.remove()`.
starter:
skills = {"python", "javascript"}

skills.add("go")
skills.add("python")  # Already there — ignored
skills.discard("javascript")

print(sorted(skills))
successMessage: add() is idempotent — adding something twice doesn't duplicate it. That's the whole point of a set.

title: Union — combine two groups
instruction:
Set operations are like Venn diagrams.

`A | B` (union) = everything from both sets combined.

Combine the frontend and backend skills.
successMessage: Union merges both sets. JavaScript appeared in both but only shows once — sets can't have duplicates.

title: Intersection — what's in common
instruction:
`A & B` (intersection) = only items in BOTH sets.

Find which skills the frontend and backend teams share.
successMessage: Intersection found the overlap. This is how recommendation engines find common interests between users.

title: Difference — what's unique to one
instruction:
`A - B` = items in A that are NOT in B.

Find which skills are unique to the frontend team.
successMessage: Difference is directional: A - B ≠ B - A. CSS is frontend-only, Python is backend-only. SQL interviews love this stuff.

title: Fast lookups — why sets matter
instruction:
Checking `item in set` is nearly instant — O(1), no matter how large the set. Checking `item in list` gets slower as the list grows — O(n).

If you're checking membership repeatedly, convert your list to a set first.
successMessage: `in` on a set is O(1) — instant lookup regardless of size. Email servers, firewalls, and spam filters all use sets for exactly this reason.

title: Build a tag analyzer 🏷️
instruction:
Two blog posts have tags. Find:
1. All unique tags across both posts
2. Tags they share
3. Tags unique to each post

Use set operations — no loops needed.
hint: Union: a | b, Intersection: a & b, Difference: a - b
starter:
post1_tags = {"python", "tutorial", "beginner", "coding"}
post2_tags = {"python", "advanced", "coding", "performance"}

all_tags = post1_tags | post2_tags
shared = post1_tags & post2_tags
only_post1 = post1_tags - post2_tags
only_post2 = post2_tags - post1_tags

print(f"All tags: {sorted(all_tags)}")
print(f"Shared: {sorted(shared)}")
print(f"Only in post 1: {sorted(only_post1)}")
print(f"Only in post 2: {sorted(only_post2)}")
successMessage: Four operations, zero loops, instant results. Sets turn complex comparisons into one-liners. That's Python.
